nodes.content: The history of AI includes myths and stories of artificial beings created by master craftsmen.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The history of artificial intelligence (AI) began in antiquity, with myths, stories, and rumors of artificial beings endowed with intelligence or consciousness by master craftsmen.

nodes.content: Transformer architecture is used alongside many generative models, contributing to the ongoing AI boom.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Transformer architecture is now used alongside many generative models that contribute to the ongoing AI boom.

nodes.content: ELMo (2018) was a bi-directional LSTM that produces contextualized word embeddings.
nodes.context_or_evidence: In language modelling, ELMo (2018) was a bi-directional LSTM that produces contextualized word embeddings, improving upon the line of research fr

nodes.content: Learning from data transformed AI, allowing systems to improve over time instead of following fixed rules.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Learning from data transformed AI, allowing systems to improve over time instead of following fixed rules.

nodes.content: Generative AI and large language models now power everyday tools, changing how people write, create, and work.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Generative AI and large language models now power everyday tools, changing how people write, create, and work.

nodes.content: Large language models are AI systems capable of understanding and generating human language by processing vast amounts of text data.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Large language models are AI systems capable of understanding and generating human language by processing vast amounts of text data.

nodes.content: Developers can use LLMs as the basis for all kinds of AI applications.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Developers can use LLMs as the basis for all kinds of AI applications.

nodes.content: AI has a long history stretching back to the 1950s.
nodes.context_or_evidence: AI has a long history stretching back to the 1950s, with significant milestones at nearly every decade.

nodes.content: The popular ChatGPT AI chatbot is a transformer-based language model based on the GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) architecture.
nodes.context_or_evidence: For example, the popular ChatGPT AI chatbot is a transformer-based language model. Specifically, it is based on the GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) archite

nodes.content: GPT-3, developed by OpenAI, is a language model capable of generating human-like text.
nodes.context_or_evidence: 2020: GPT-3, developed by OpenAI, is a powerful language model capable of generating human-like text.

nodes.content: GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters.
nodes.context_or_evidence: It has 175 billion parameters, making it one of the largest and most sophisticated AI language models at its release.

nodes.content: Domains like example.com and example.org are maintained for documentation purposes and are not available for registration or transfer.
nodes.context_or_evidence: As described in RFC 2606 and RFC 6761, these domains are used as illustrative examples in documents without prior coordination with IANA.

nodes.content: IANA provides a web service on the example domain hosts to provide basic information on the purpose of the domain.
nodes.context_or_evidence: These web services are provided as best effort and are not designed to support production applications. Applications should not be designed to require the example domains to have operating HTTP service.

nodes.content: The Transformer model utilizes a self-attention mechanism to understand the context and relationships between words in a sequence.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The self-attention mechanism allows the model to look at other words in the input sequence as it encodes a specific word.

nodes.content: Self-attention enables a model to simultaneously assess all positions within a sequence to determine the most relevant words for encoding meaning.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The paper's key innovation is self-attention, a mechanism that allows a model to look at all positions in a sequence simultaneously and determine which words matter most when encoding meaning.

nodes.content: Self-attention can be viewed as a directed graph.
nodes.context_or_evidence: In this post, I'll clearly explain self-attention & how it can be thought of as a directed graph.

nodes.content: Self-attention, also known as intra-attention, relates different positions of a single sequence to compute a representation of the sequence.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Self-attention, sometimes called intra-attention is an attention mechanism relating different positions of a single sequence in order to compute a representation of the sequence.

nodes.content: In self-attention, each token in a sequence can simultaneously 'look' at every other token, revealing contextual relationships.
nodes.context_or_evidence: In simplest terms, self-attention is a mechanism by which every token in a sequence (e.g., a word or subword) can "look" at every other token simultaneously, discovering contextual relationships without being forced to crawl step-by-step through the data.

nodes.content: The Transformer architecture revolutionized generative AI and powers large language models (LLMs).
nodes.context_or_evidence: The course description for "How Transformer LLMs Work" states that the transformer architecture is fundamental to generative AI and is the basis for LLMs.

nodes.content: The 'GPT' in ChatGPT stands for 'Generative Pre-Trained Transformer'.
nodes.context_or_evidence: This is stated in the course description for "How Transformer LLMs Work" to highlight the importance of the Transformer architecture.

nodes.content: The Transformer architecture was introduced in the 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' by Ashish Vaswani and others.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The course description references the original paper that introduced the Transformer architecture.

nodes.content: The Transformer model was initially designed as a highly scalable model for machine translation tasks.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The course description mentions that the Transformer architecture, introduced in the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, was initially intended for machine translation.

nodes.content: The Transformer dispenses with recurrence and convolutions entirely, relying solely on attention mechanisms.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper states that the Transformer architecture is based solely on attention mechanisms, removing the need for recurrence and convolutions.

nodes.content: The Transformer model achieved a BLEU score of 28.4 on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, surpassing existing results by over 2 BLEU.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper reports the performance of the Transformer model on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task.

nodes.content: The Transformer model achieved a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.0 on the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task after training for 3.5 days on eight GPUs.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper reports the performance of the Transformer model on the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, including training time and hardware.

nodes.content: Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), particularly LSTMs and gated recurrent networks, were the state-of-the-art approaches in sequence modeling and transduction problems before the Transformer.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The introduction of the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper states that RNNs were the dominant approach before the Transformer.

nodes.content: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is used to train Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) for learning sequential data.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The article explains the intricate details of the BPTT algorithm and how it is used for training RNNs.

nodes.content: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is a fundamental technique used to train Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks.
nodes.context_or_evidence: LSTMs are designed to handle long-term dependencies and mitigate the vanishing gradient problem, and BPTT is used in their training.

nodes.content: Gradients obtained by backpropagation are used with gradient-based techniques to train an RNN, and this process is called BPTT (Back Propagation Through Time).
nodes.context_or_evidence: The document mentions that Gradient Descent uses gradients obtained by backpropagation and refers to this as BPTT.

nodes.content: Self-attention mechanisms in Transformer architectures use a scaling factor of √dₖ.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The article discusses the reason behind scaling attention by √dₖ in Transformers, exploring the math, intuition, and role of softmax in self-attention.

nodes.content: The Transformer architecture was introduced in a 2017 paper titled 'Attention is All You Need'.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The article mentions the 2017 paper that introduced the transformer architecture, which led to the development of Large Language Models.

nodes.content: Self-Attention mechanism is included in the Encoder block of the Transformer architecture.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The text mentions the Self-Attention mechanism as part of the Encoder block in the Transformer architecture, followed by processing by fully connected layers.

nodes.content: The Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA) operates and maintains key aspects of the Domain Name System (DNS).
nodes.context_or_evidence: IANA operates and maintains aspects of the DNS, including the root zone, and the .int and .arpa domains.

nodes.content: The DNS root zone involves delegating administrative responsibility of top-level domains (TLDs).
nodes.context_or_evidence: The root is the upper-most part of the DNS hierarchy, and involves delegating administrative responsibility of top-level domains, such as .com, .uk and .nz.

nodes.content: Residual connections and layer normalization are essential for training deep transformer networks because they address the vanishing gradient problem and training instability.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Residual connections and layer normalization act as safety features in a car, preventing vanishing gradients and training instability, which are critical problems in deep transformer networks.

nodes.content: Residual connections, also known as skip connections, and layer normalization are techniques used within the Transformer architecture to address issues and enable the training of multi-layered models.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The text explicitly states that residual connections (skip connections) and layer normalization are used in the Transformer architecture to solve problems and allow for training of multi-layered models.

nodes.content: Transformers can be categorized into Post-LN and Pre-LN architectures based on the position of layer normalization (LN).
nodes.context_or_evidence: The architecture of Transformers can be classified as either Post-LN or Pre-LN depending on where the layer normalization is positioned within the network.

nodes.content: Recent Transformers prefer Pre-LN architectures because training Post-LN architectures with deep Transformers (e.g., ten or more layers) is difficult.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The text indicates that Pre-LN architectures are favored in recent Transformer designs due to the challenges associated with training deep Post-LN Transformers, specifically those with ten or more layers.

nodes.content: There is a residual connection around each of the two sub-layers in the encoder and decoder stacks, followed by layer normalization.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Each sub-layer in the encoder and decoder stacks of a Transformer model is surrounded by a residual connection, and this is followed by layer normalization.

nodes.content: Residual connections and layer normalization are implemented in the AddAndNorm module and applied after each sub-layer in both the encoder and decoder.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The AddAndNorm module is responsible for implementing residual connections and layer normalization, and it is applied after every sub-layer in both the encoder and decoder components of the Transformer architecture.

nodes.content: A modified Transformer architecture locates the layer normalization inside the residual blocks.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The text mentions an alternative Transformer architecture design where the layer normalization is positioned within the residual blocks.

nodes.content: The vanishing gradient problem is an issue in training neural networks with backpropagation, where gradient magnitudes diverge significantly between earlier and later layers.
nodes.context_or_evidence: This problem occurs when using backpropagation to train neural networks, leading to substantial differences in gradient magnitudes across different layers.

nodes.content: Backpropagation through time (BPTT) suffers from gradient decay that is exponentially proportional to the number of time steps or layers traversed.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Diplom thesis proved that BPTT experiences gradient decay that increases exponentially with the number of time steps or layers.

nodes.content: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) struggle to remember information from earlier steps as sequences grow longer, due to the vanishing gradient problem.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The vanishing gradient problem limits RNNs' ability to understand long-term dependencies in tasks like machine translation, making them less effective for long sequences.

nodes.content: Vanishing gradients can cripple RNNs by making it nearly impossible for them to learn long-term patterns.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Gradients shrink exponentially during backpropagation, especially in long sequences or when using certain activation functions like sigmoid, which makes it difficult for RNNs to learn long-term patterns.

nodes.content: During Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) over long sequences, the gradient is multiplied by the weight matrix and tanh derivative at each step.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The gradient is repeatedly multiplied by the weight matrix and the derivative of the tanh activation function during BPTT, which can lead to the vanishing gradient problem.

nodes.content: Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) mitigate the vanishing gradient problem more effectively than traditional RNNs, allowing them to capture longer-term dependencies in data.
nodes.context_or_evidence: GRUs are designed to address the vanishing gradient problem, enabling them to learn and retain information over longer sequences compared to standard RNNs.

nodes.content: Weight initialization is one technique developed to reduce the vanishing gradient problem in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs).
nodes.context_or_evidence: Specific weight initialization strategies can help to alleviate the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs.

nodes.content: Viso Suite is an end-to-end computer vision infrastructure designed for building, deploying, and scaling AI vision applications.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Viso Suite provides a comprehensive platform for developing and implementing computer vision solutions.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 defines what it means for a Domain Name (DNS name) to be reserved for special use.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 6761 states that the document describes the meaning of reserving a Domain Name for special use, when it is appropriate, and the procedure for doing so.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 establishes an IANA registry for special-use domain names.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 6761 states that it establishes an IANA registry for such domain names, and seeds it with entries for some of the already established special domain names.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 updates RFC 1918 and RFC 2606.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The header of RFC 6761 indicates that it updates RFC 1918 and RFC 2606.

nodes.content: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) were used for sequence modeling and generation for many years.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The Wikipedia article on Transformers states that for many years, sequence modeling and generation was done by using plain recurrent neural networks (RNNs).

nodes.content: Elman network (1990) is a well-cited early example of RNNs used for sequence modeling.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The Wikipedia article on Transformers mentions the Elman network (1990) as a well-cited early example of RNNs.

nodes.content: LSTM networks are an improved version of RNNs designed to solve the vanishing gradient problem.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The GeeksforGeeks article states that LSTM networks are an improved version of RNNs designed to solve the vanishing gradient problem.

nodes.content: LSTMs use memory cells to keep information over longer periods.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The GeeksforGeeks article mentions that LSTMs use memory cells that keep information over longer periods.

nodes.content: LSTMs have special gates to control the flow of information.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The GeeksforGeeks article states that LSTMs have special gates to control the flow of information.

nodes.content: Simple RNN architectures have limitations in modeling sequences effectively, especially when long-term patterns are present, due to gradient problems.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The apxml.com article states that gradient problems fundamentally limit the ability of simple RNN architectures to model sequences effectively, especially when long-term patterns are present.

nodes.content: RNNs are particularly effective in capturing short-term dependencies in sequences.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The Baeldung on Computer Science article states that RNNs are particularly effective in capturing short-term dependencies in sequences.

nodes.content: RNNs suffer from the vanishing gradient problem, where the influence of earlier inputs diminishes exponentially as the sequence progresses.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The Baeldung on Computer Science article mentions that RNNs suffer from the vanishing gradient problem, where the influence of earlier inputs diminishes exponentially as the sequence progresses.

nodes.content: LSTMs use additive state updates rather than purely multiplicative transitions, enabling gradients to flow backward through time more effectively.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The jacksteve-code.github.io article states that unlike vanilla RNNs, LSTMs use additive state updates rather than purely multiplicative transitions, enabling gradients to flow backward through time more effectively.

nodes.content: RFC 2606 specifies Internet Best Current Practices for the Internet Community.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The document itself states that it specifies an Internet Best Current Practices and requests discussion and suggestions for improvements, with distribution of the memo being unlimited.

nodes.content: RFC 2606 reserves a few top-level domain names for use in private testing and documentation.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 2606 states that to reduce the likelihood of conflict and confusion, a few top-level domain names are reserved for use in private testing, as examples in documentation, and the like.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 updates RFC 1918 and RFC 2606.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The RFC 6761 information page on the RFC Editor website explicitly states that it updates RFC 1918 and RFC 2606.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 describes what it means for a Domain Name (DNS name) to be reserved for special use.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 6761 states that the document describes what it means to say that a Domain Name (DNS name) is reserved for special use, when reserving such a name is appropriate, and the procedure for doing so.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 establishes an IANA registry for special-use domain names.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 6761 states that the document establishes an IANA registry for such domain names.

nodes.content: The RFC series is identified by ISSN 2070-1721.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The RFC Editor website states that the RFC Series (ISSN 2070-1721) contains technical and organizational documents about the Internet.

nodes.content: The RFC series includes documents produced by the IETF, IRTF, and IAB.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The RFC Editor website states that the RFC Series contains documents produced by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF), the Internet Research Task Force (IRTF), and the Internet Architecture Board (IAB).

nodes.content: RFC 6761 describes what it means for a Domain Name (DNS name) to be reserved for special use, when reserving such a name is appropriate, and the procedure for doing so.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 6761 explicitly states its purpose is to define special-use domain names and the process for reserving them.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 establishes an IANA registry for special-use domain names and seeds it with entries for already established special domain names.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The abstract of RFC 6761 states that it establishes an IANA registry.

nodes.content: RFC 6761 updates RFC 1918 and RFC 2606.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The header of RFC 6761 indicates that it updates RFC 1918 and RFC 2606.

nodes.content: Residual connections in deep neural networks enable gradients to flow through identity shortcuts, reducing the vanishing gradient problem.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Multiple sources (GeeksforGeeks, mbrenndoerfer.com) explicitly state that residual connections allow gradients to flow through identity shortcuts, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

nodes.content: Residual connections simplify training by learning residual functions instead of full mappings.
nodes.context_or_evidence: GeeksforGeeks article on Residual Networks states that identity mapping simplifies training by learning residual functions.

nodes.content: For optimal gradient flow in deep residual networks, the shortcut should be a pure identity mapping without modifications.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Piyushjajoo.github.io explicitly states that for optimal gradient flow, the shortcut should be a pure identity mapping.

nodes.content: Deep residual networks can suffer from gradient overlap due to interactions between gradients from the learned transformation and the identity mapping (skip connections) during backpropagation.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The arxiv.org paper 'Mitigating Gradient Overlap in Deep Residual Networks with Gradient ...' explicitly states the problem of gradient overlap due to interactions between gradients from the learned transformation and the identity mapping.

nodes.content: IETF is the Internet Engineering Task Force.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The IETF website's title and logo clearly indicate that IETF stands for Internet Engineering Task Force.

nodes.content: Adding more layers to neural networks was initially believed to improve model performance by enriching extracted features, as seen with VGG16 and VGG19.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The assumption was that deeper networks could extract more complex and informative features, leading to better performance. VGG16 and VGG19 are cited as examples of networks where increasing depth was initially associated with improved results.

nodes.content: The degradation problem emerged as a challenge in training very deep neural networks, where accuracy saturated and then rapidly decreased as network depth increased.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Experiments comparing shallow and deep plain networks showed that deeper models exhibited higher training and test errors. This degradation was not due to overfitting but to an increase in training error as the network became deeper, indicating that the added layers failed to approximate the identity function.

nodes.content: ResNet's residual connections enabled the training of extremely deep networks, leading to improved accuracy compared to previous architectures.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Residual connections allow the network to learn residuals (the difference between the learned representation and the input) instead of directly mapping inputs to outputs. This is achieved through skip connections (or shortcut connections).

nodes.content: Residual connections in ResNets facilitate gradient flow through identity shortcuts, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks.
nodes.context_or_evidence: Residual connections create an identity path from outputs back to earlier layers, allowing gradients to flow more easily through very deep networks.

nodes.content: Residual mappings in ResNets can more easily learn the identity function, such as by pushing parameters in the weight layer to zero.
nodes.context_or_evidence: By learning residual functions instead of full mappings, ResNets simplify training and allow for the creation of effective deep neural networks.

nodes.content: IETF's Nominating Committee (NomCom) is part of the structure of the IETF.
nodes.context_or_evidence: The IETF website lists the Nominating Committee as one of the structural components of the IETF, alongside the IESG, IAB, IRTF, IETF Trust, and RFC Editor.
